title: Booleans & Logic
subtitle: Teach the computer to answer yes-or-no questions.
goal: Understand True and False, compare values with == != < > <= >=, and combine yes/no questions using and, or, and not.

blocks.heading: Yes or No? 🤔
blocks.html:
Lots of questions in life have only two answers: yes or no. Is it raining? Are you 10 years old? Is 5 bigger than 3?

Python has a special way to say yes and no. It uses two magic words: True (yes) and False (no). We call these booleans. Notice the capital letters — True and False always start big!

blocks.caption: True and False are real values
blocks.code:
is_raining = True
is_sunny = False
print(is_raining)
print(is_sunny)
blocks.explain: True means yes, False means no. They are values you can store in a variable, just like numbers and text. Always capital T and capital F.

blocks.heading: Asking comparison questions
blocks.html:
You can make Python answer questions by comparing two things. Each question gives back True or False.

  • == are they equal?
  • != are they NOT equal?
  • < less than   > greater than
  • <= less or equal   >= greater or equal
Super important! One equals sign = means assign (put this value in the box). Two equals signs == means compare (are these the same?). Mixing them up is the most common beginner mistake!

blocks.caption: Let Python answer some questions
blocks.code:
print(5 > 3)
print(2 == 2)
print(7 != 7)
print(10 <= 4)
age = 10
print(age >= 10)
blocks.explain: Each comparison prints True or False. 5 > 3 is True. 7 != 7 is False because 7 IS equal to 7. age >= 10 is True because 10 is 'greater than or equal to' 10.

blocks.heading: Combining questions: and, or, not 🔗
blocks.html:
Real life questions are often bigger: Is it raining AND cold? Python lets you join questions together.

  • and → True only when both sides are True
  • or → True when at least one side is True
  • not → flips it: not True becomes False

Here is a tiny truth table for and. Think of it like needing BOTH a ticket AND a snack to enjoy the movie:

LeftRightLeft and Right
TrueTrueTrue ✅
TrueFalseFalse
FalseTrueFalse
FalseFalseFalse

With or you only need ONE side to be True — like getting ice cream if it's hot OR if it's your birthday. 🍦

blocks.caption: Joining yes/no questions
blocks.code:
is_raining = True
is_cold = False
print(is_raining and is_cold)
print(is_raining or is_cold)
print(not is_raining)
temperature = 30
print(temperature > 25 and temperature < 40)
blocks.explain: is_raining and is_cold is False because is_cold is False (and needs BOTH). is_raining or is_cold is True because one side is True. not is_raining flips True into False. The last line is True because 30 is between 25 and 40.

blocks.html: Sneaky truthiness: Python treats some empty things as 'no'. The number 0, empty text "", and an empty list count as falsy (like False). Almost everything else counts as truthy (like True). You'll use this more in later lessons!

blocks.question: What does 4 == 4 print?
blocks.explain: == asks 'are these equal?'. 4 really does equal 4, so the answer is True.

blocks.question: You want to check if two things are the SAME. Which symbol do you use?
blocks.explain: Use == to compare. One = is for assigning a value to a variable, not for asking a question.

blocks.question: If it is raining is True and it is cold is False, what is (raining and cold)?
options: False, because 'and' needs BOTH to be True
blocks.explain: The word 'and' only gives True when both sides are True. Since cold is False, the whole thing is False.

blocks.title: Compare two numbers
blocks.instructions: Make Python print whether 12 is greater than 7. Use the > symbol so it prints True. Then press Check.
blocks.starter:
# TODO: use > so this prints True
print(12 < 7)
blocks.failMsg: Replace the blank with > so the line reads print(12 > 7).

blocks.title: Is it picnic weather?
blocks.instructions: We have it as sunny (True) and warm (True). Print whether it is sunny AND warm using the word 'and'. It should print True.
blocks.starter:
is_sunny = True
is_warm = True
# TODO: combine the two with the word  and
print(is_sunny)
blocks.solution:
is_sunny = True
is_warm = True
print(is_sunny and is_warm)
blocks.passMsg: Perfect picnic weather — both questions were True!
blocks.failMsg: Use the word 'and' between is_sunny and is_warm.

challenge.title: Can I ride the roller coaster?
challenge.instructions: A roller coaster has a rule: you can ride if you are AT LEAST 8 years old AND your height is MORE THAN 120 cm. Set age = 10 and height = 130, then print True or False for whether this person can ride. Use 'and' to join the two checks!
challenge.starter:
age = 10
height = 130
# TODO: use  and  to check age >= 8 AND height > 120
print(age >= 8)
challenge.hint: Use >= for 'at least 8', use > for 'more than 120', and join them with the word 'and'.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You did it! Both rules were True, so they can ride. You've mastered booleans and logic — perfect timing, because Day 7 uses these to make REAL decisions with if-statements!
